I decided against `get_cached_distribution` for two reasons: 

First, there seems to be no canonical per-user cache location like `rosdep` has it, so I would end up storing the cache in a `catkin_lint` specific location and be unable to take advantage of pre-cached data from other programs.

Second, as I understand the code, it merely downloads the package manifests one by one, which I can do myself more efficiently by limiting the download to those packages which I actually need (I only use `rosdistro` as a last resort if no local information is available, as I feel that the locally installed packages should override the official distribution sources anyway).

Regarding the cache invalidation, I will implement an `--update-rosdistro` just to be safe, although I don't expect it will be needed very often: the `rosdistro` index itself is not cached and will be fetched each time `catkin_lint` wants to download data, so the cache merely prevents `catkin_lint` from learning about changes in previously downloaded manifests.

Building `HTSeq` reports an error `Failed building wheel for htseq`. The same goes for `pysam`.

Use the latest version of `HTSeq` - we want the latest version of all bioinformatics tools in this Docker image. For older versions we have the `resolwebio/rnaseq:legacy` Docker image.

Installation of `HTSeq` with `pip` makes `htseq-count` available on `$PATH`. Having two versions of `HTSeq` installed in the same Docker image makes it difficult to control which version is called in bash from [htseq-count process](https://github.com/genialis/resolwe-bio/blob/master/resolwe_bio/processes/expression/htseq_count.yml#L147). For this reason I dislike the idea of having both versions installed in the same Docker image.

I see two solutions: (1) The [script](https://github.com/vivekbhr/Subread_to_DEXSeq/blob/master/dexseq_prepare_annotation2.py) that requires the Python 2 version of `HTSeq` is really easy to update for Python3. The `DEXSeq` manual even provides a detailed explanation of this script in case you want to modify it or write your own. Only two changes are required: `raise ValueError` needs to be called with parentheses and `xrange` needs to be replaced with `range`. You can also make a pull request in that repository. (2) Use a separate Docker image.

Hi @wbolster, as you can see from this thread, this is an issue that has been around for a few years.  Actually, it's been known (to at least me) for almost the entire life of `dill` (10 years).  

I've known the "backport" solution (basically, what is done in `qualname` and thus what is done in python 3) since `__qualname__` appeared in python 3 -- however, I wasn't able to resolve the issues that `qualname` avoids… so I don't have code in `dill` to handle it at all yet.

`dill.source` enables source introspection (like the `inspect` module), with support for objects defined in `__main__` as well as extracting source (or imports) for dependencies so an object can be rebuilt from the source.  A  blend of some of the functions available in `dill.source` that can dig into an object's source code with the root code from `qualname`, I believe, would cover many of the cases that `qualname` currently fails on.  At this point, I'm not suggesting augmenting `qualname`, but instead extending `dill` using `dill.source` and some of the code inside `qualname`… however, if I find that it'd be better to go the inverse route (extending `qualname` not `dill.source`) I will submit a patch when I get to this issue.

Hey @baniobloom - troposphere is basically just a python implementation of CloudFormation, with some added benefits.  That means that anything you read in the CloudFormation documentation can be done in troposphere.  But at it's core, that's just creating the CloudFormation templates.

There is the 'cfn' tool, which can be used to take those templates and push them into Cloudformation, bringing up the stacks defined in the templates.  You should check out the help on that tool.

Another project that handles more of the higher level things is stacker - you can read more about it here: http://stacker.readthedocs.org/en/latest/ and here's the github repo: https://github.com/remind101/stacker

Resolving in favor of #4102

> Why do we build suffixed and unsuffixed versions, rather than just always using a suffixed version, not allowing overwrites, and having the release use the existing suffixed version if it exists? That way we can shave off the 45 minute delay (assuming the commit you're tagging has already been built).

The issue with that approach (on the surface), is that using suffixed releases in all cases would mean we were pushing suffixed releases to pypi... and what we want to be pushing are stable releases. 

But in theory we could "re-version" the whls when we were ready to release them using a stable version... I talked with @kwlzn about this, and in theory it should be possible by renaming a few files in the whl, updating the introduced `src/python/pants/VERSION` file, and updating all of their hashes. I don't really like the idea of cracking open the whl files though.

> Where do the wheels get stored? Do we prune them at some point? Moving from "100M per release" to "100M per commit" seems like a non-trivial storage increase.

In S3. Haven't looked at our bill recently, but I don't expect it to add up to a noticeable amount anytime soon.

thanks for the config! This is indeed an edge case cause by having relatively short sequences and a large standard deviation of the length ratios between the source and target. During inference when you use learned positional embeddings we need to make sure that neither the source nor the target side ever exceed the maximum length (in your case that's 5 on the source side and 4 on the target side. In order to do so the maximum input length is calculated as:
```
max_output_len = 5 - 2 # namely the maximum output length minus space for special characters
factor = length_ratio_mean + num_stds * length_ratio_std # 0.9895764162499294 + 2 * 0.7557519252689273
max_input_len = int(floor(max_output_len / factor))
```
In your case this leads to `max_input_len = 0`.

So for the short term solution you can either:
1.  Use the fixed embeddings as suggested by Felix (`--cnn-positional-embedding-type fixed`).
2. At inference time you could use `--max-output-length-num-stds 0`. However this would lead to `max_input_len=2` and not help during training, so that's suboptimal.

As a fix we should at least through a nicer error message in case this happens and we could potentially add a different mode for setting the maximum and minimum length not based on the ratio, but rather to the minimum and maximum supported in the case we run into these edge cases.

Ran into this as well, dug a little deeper. It looks like the ffmpeg plugin is not properly cleaning up the processes it spawns. Issuing a hard `imageio.proc.kill()` after you are done with your logic will kill the process spawned by Popen effectively solving your problem. I've tried to figure out how to patch the ffmpeg plugin, but didn't see a quick fix, issuing a self.proc.kill() in the terminate did not work for some reason.

* make sure that imageio accepts `None` for the quantizer for all gif plugins.

* for the (current default) Pillow plugin, by far the best quantizer seems to be '0'. Apparently, I did not even bother documenting the quantizer argument.

It is a bit difficult to test the PR, primarily due to the fact that I don't have access to the configuration of the continuous integration servers. 

I work on a project called [Menpo](http://www.menpo.org), and we use a small script hosted [here](https://github.com/jabooth/condaci/).

We host the conda build scripts inside the main python repository, and then we essentially just run [this command](https://github.com/menpo/menpo/blob/master/.travis.yml#L22), which does some work to find out if the current build is a tag or not. If it is a tag, it uploads it to binstar automatically. Otherwise, it uploads every build to a special channel called 'master` that hosts the latest passing build.

The only thing that really changes is the manner in which the `meta.yaml` works out what version to build. We use the `CONDACI_VERSION` environment variable to specify this [in the `meta.yaml`](https://github.com/menpo/menpo/blob/master/conda/meta.yaml#L3).

The last thing to mention is that you need to set up your Binstar key as encrypted variables inside your Appveyor and Travis builds.

> In the pmml file, those dummy variables which had all zero values do not appear in the datadictionary or miningmodel sections.

My PMML conversion tools, including SkLearn2PMML/JPMML-SkLearn, perform several PMML "tidying" actions. One of the tidying actions is deleting all unused `DataDictionary/DataField`, `TransformationDictionary/DerivedField` (and the corresponding `MiningSchema/MiningField`) elements. Why keep those dummy features, if they are not needed for model scoring? They only increase the size of the PMML document, and make it harder to read and analyze for humans.

 > However, when I use random forest in this package, all those zero value dummy variables would be included.

If a feature is included, then it is used by the model - your dummy feature wasn't so dummy after all (maybe it's 99% zero values, and 1% one values, and the latter are really helpful in explaining the variance).

The difference between selected feature sets between XGBoost and Random Forest comes from their algorithmic difference - XGBoost picks the next feature very carefully (prioritizing high-info features over low-info features), whereas Random Forest picks the next feature pretty much randomly (thereby increasing the chance that low-info features are selected).

> I am wondering if there is anything I can do to fix it (via adding lines in the pmml file).

This is not an error (it simply reflects the different logic XGBoost and Random Forest algorithms), and it does not need to be fixed - both PMML documents are valid/correct.

I'm reopening this issue, because there needs to be support for feature selection in PMML conversion workflows.

If the feature selection happens in transformed feature space (eg. after categorical columns have been expanded using `OneHotEncoder` transformer), then it's virtually impossible to backtrack it in Scikit-Learn application code. However, it's pretty straightforward in JPMML-SkLearn.

> Short answer: Python-for-Android converts a numeric period-separated version into the "pure integer" version that Android (and the Play Store) need. This resulting number needs to be monotonically increasing otherwise you can't install on top of an older version

Thanks for explaining @jamalex 

@christianmemije - the version scheme as described by @jamalex is ideally property of the installer build and not the source distribution. For instance, you may change a build but base it on identical source dist versions. You could also say that we have separate release flows: 1) For the source dist 2) For the Android installer (based on the source dist)

Thus, basing this on the number of git commits as seen by the source dist wouldn't do all the work.

An example is the filenames of our Debian packages: `kolibri-0.6.0-0ubuntu0.deb`. Essentially, we add information about the build number to the versioning, because we may change things in the installer, but still base it on the same source release.

I see that we might be able to explore this to achieve a unique versioning scheme for the Android release without touching our own source code base.....

http://python-for-android.readthedocs.io/en/latest/distutils/

> All of these automatic arguments can be overridden by passing them manually on the command line, e.g.:
> 
>     python setup.py apk --name="Testapp Setup" --version=2.5

So I was thinking something like for the build command, then we don't get installer-specific stuff in the source dist:

>     kolibri_major_version=`python -c "import kolibri; print('.'.join(map(str, kolibri.VERSION[:3])))"`
>     kolibri_commit_count=`cd kolibri; git rev-list --count HEAD`
>     android_build_commit_count=`git rev-list --count HEAD`
>     android_version="$kolibri_version.$kolibri_commit_count.$android_build_commit_count"
>     
>     python kolibri/setup.py apk --name="Testapp Setup" --version=$android_version

Hey, I just wanted to confirm that the extended time to produce these reports is reproducible on my end for your account. The request is generated and sent to the endpoint, but the endpoint doesn't respond for an extended amount of time. That said, it does eventually download.

I'm pretty confident that you're correct about hitting some sort of API limitation; e.g. the API responds relatively quickly when the number of fields is reduced significantly and slows down as more are added. In the case where the report is "stuck" for you, the time needed to generate the large report with all of those fields is significantly longer. It does complete if given enough time, so I'm not convinced that having a shorter timeout hard-coded into the library is actually desirable here. Does that seem reasonable?

This issue tracker is intended for discussion about this particular client library, but there core issue here--i.e. the delay when generating a large report--appears to be related to the API itself. If you want to go into more detail on that topic, I suggest reaching out on the [AdWords support forum](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/adwords-api).

Regards,
Mark

Here is some simple benchmarks (not terribly scientific)..

[nnpybench.pdf](https://github.com/nanomsg/nnpy/files/478926/nnpybench.pdf)

Using this test code:

```python
import time
import nnpy
import sys

PACKETS = 50000
MSG = b'\xff' * 1024


def echo(req, reply):
    req.send(MSG)
    got = reply.recv()
    assert got == MSG, got
    reply.send(got)
    got = req.recv()
    assert got == MSG, got


def setup(addr):
    req = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.REQ)
    req.bind(addr)
    reply = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.REP)
    reply.connect(addr)
    return req, reply


def test(req, reply):
    for i in range(PACKETS):
        echo(req, reply)


for addr in 'tcp://127.0.0.1:6423', 'inproc://foo', 'ipc://bar':
    req, reply = setup(addr)
    time.sleep(0.500)
    start = time.time()
    try:
        test(req, reply)
    except Exception as e:
        print("%20s, %25s,,,FAILED,%s" % (sys.argv[1], addr, e))
    else:
        took = time.time() - start
        pps = PACKETS / took
        print("%20s, %25s, %f, %d" % (sys.argv[1], addr, took, round(pps)))
```

And these two patches

`instance_check` patch
```patch
diff --git a/nnpy/socket.py b/nnpy/socket.py
index 2ce24d3..37bec69 100644
--- a/nnpy/socket.py
+++ b/nnpy/socket.py
@@ -67,7 +67,7 @@ class Socket(object):
         return errors.convert(rc, rc)
     
     def send(self, data, flags=0):
-        if isinstance(data, memoryview):
+        if isinstance(data, (memoryview, bytes)):
             buf = ffi.from_buffer(data)
         else:
             data = data.encode() if isinstance(data, ustr) else data
```


`try_from_buffer_first` patch
```patch
diff --git a/nnpy/socket.py b/nnpy/socket.py
index 2ce24d3..10d4d72 100644
--- a/nnpy/socket.py
+++ b/nnpy/socket.py
@@ -67,9 +67,9 @@ class Socket(object):
         return errors.convert(rc, rc)
     
     def send(self, data, flags=0):
-        if isinstance(data, memoryview):
+        try:
             buf = ffi.from_buffer(data)
-        else:
+        except TypeError:
             data = data.encode() if isinstance(data, ustr) else data
             buf = ffi.new('char[%i]' % len(data), data)
         rc = nanomsg.nn_send(self.sock, buf, len(data), flags)

> I think the correct behaviour here is for dry-run to check if you've passed a vocab_dir - if you have and it is empty, we continue as normal - if you have and it has a vocab in it, we copy the vocab to a original_vocab directory, and extend the vocabulary with the instances in the dataset. That way, you get no surprises if you didn't want the vocab extended, but it does the most useful thing in most circumstances.

This kind of specific logic wouldn't be the same as how `train` (and now `make-vocab`) decides how to construct vocab to use. Users do have specific control on how to extend vocabulary by explicitly configuring it. So such implicit choices based on directory is empty or not might be confusing. Instead, I think it would be better to leave that logic on a one common interpretation of vocab_params across all commands.

Crudely after (#1416), this is how final vocabulary is decided in `train`
```
If vocabulary[directory_path] is passed:
	If vocabulary[extend] is True
		final_vocab => Load vocabulary[directory_path] and extend it with dataset instances.
	Else:
		final_vocab => Load vocabulary[directory_path]
Else:
	final_vocab => Build from dataset instances
```

More specifically, 4 possibilities of **INPUT** interpretations of vocab_params are:

**A.**: final-vocabulary => load "presaved_vocab", *extend with* dataset instances.
```json
"vocabulary": {
	"directory_path": "presaved_vocab",
	"extend": true
}

```

**B.**: final-vocabulary => load "presaved_vocab"
```json
"vocabulary": {
	"directory_path": "presaved_vocab",
	"extend": false
}

```

**C.**: final-vocabulary => build from tokens in dataset instances.
```json
"vocabulary": {
	"extend": false
}

```

**D.**: Error: Asked to extend but no vocabulary passed to extend it from.
```json
"vocabulary": {
	"extend": true
}

```
"extend": False is default in all cases.


For all these choices, the decided final-vocabulary (**OUTPUT**) is saved in `serialization_dir/vocabulary` irrespective of what vocab_params is.

This gives a good control on making, composing multiple vocabularies by explicitly configuring. So I think decisions like: when to extend, how to extend, what is the final vocabulary used and dumped and where is it dumped should be left out on this only (in all of `train`, `make-vocab`, `dry-run` commands). 

After this PR, For **all** 3 commands:
1. (**INPUT**): we leave vocab_params interpretation fully and only to `Vocabulary.from_params(...)` **instead of**:
   a. pre-popping sub params to make certain decisions (*as in make-vocab and dry-run*)
   b. using something else like `verbosely_create_vocabulary(..)` (*as in dry-run*)
2. (**OUTPUT**) dump the final vocabulary used in "serialization_dir/vocabulary".

Something that we discussed (without deciding whether or not it's a good idea) is using the config file as a temporary storage solution between runs of commands.
We could also do this with a file in `/tmp/` if that feels cleaner.

That lets us add a feature for storing and retrieving values, which can substitute themselves for repetitive or often-used options.
We can use a syntax similar to the `globus config set` syntax to handle specification.

Consider the case of `globus transfer ls`:

Without tooling, it's
```shell
globus transfer ls --endpoint-id 'aabbccdd' --path '/x/'
globus transfer ls --endpoint-id 'aabbccdd' --path '/x/y/'
globus transfer ls --endpoint-id 'aabbccdd' --path '/x/y/z'
```

But it could be
```shell
globus set transfer.endpoint-id 'aabbccdd'
globus transfer ls --path '/x/'
globus transfer ls --path '/x/y/'
globus transfer ls --path '/x/y/z'
```

Or we can do this where `--endpoint-id` is optional, but `--path` is converted into a positional:
```shell
globus transfer ls --endpoint-id 'aabbccdd' '/x/y/z'
```
becomes
```shell
globus set transfer.endpoint-id 'aabbccdd'
globus transfer ls '/x/y/z'
```

And we also have
```shell
$ globus clear
$ globus transfer ls '/x/y/z'
Usage Error: "globus transfer ls" requires "--endpoint-id"
```

This feature appeals extremely strongly to me, adding `globus set`, `globus show`, and `globus clear` as our three commands.
For `ls` in particular, the notion of setting an endpoint to operate on is much more comfortable than repeatedly specifying it -- positionally or as an option.

Importantly, the semantics only remain clear if the values being set via `globus set` are options.
Otherwise, the command `globus transfer ls aabbccdd` doesn't parse unambiguously.

Hi @achourasia,

In order to establish a session with the CLI you have to go through the browser login flow at least once. After that it should continue to work with no human intervention as long as a command is run at least once every 6 months (the underlying refresh tokens will expire if not used once every 6 months).

If you absolutely cannot go through the browser login flow, you might want to consider using our SDK (Python), which can be found at https://github.com/globus/globus-sdk-python. You would be able to use something called the [client credentials grant](http://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/examples/client_credentials/) which is a way for an application to authenticate "as itself" for situations where it doesn't work to require a human to log in.

:+1: on reducing to a single `encode()` method.

I slightly prefer the `('int', 'int')` format for the ABI types argument. It's parallel to the way the values are being passed in. The tuple format is used in a few places in web3, like (pseudocode):

```py
types = get_abi_types()
vals = get_vals()
encoded = encode_abi(types, vals)
```

Which is made less parallel, and a tiny bit more awkward, with:

```py
types = get_abi_types()
vals = get_vals()
joint_types = '(%s)' % ','.join(types)
encoded = encode_abi(joint_types, vals)
```

I'm just going to share what I learned these last few weeks:

  * conda [outputs](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/build-packages/define-metadata.html#outputs-section) probably do what you want in terms of having a single recipe. The cost to pay will be approximately equal to an "additional tab" and the choice of a naming convention. I'll try to put an example up for `scikit-image` in the next few weeks. For example, you might have the following outputs section
```yaml
outputs:
  - name: scikit-image
    # Most of your original recipe would go here.
  - name: scikit-image.test
    requirements:
       run:
       - {{ pin_subpackage('scikit-image', exact=True) }}
       - pytest
  - name: scikit-image.docs
    requirements:
       run:
       - {{ pin_subpackage('scikit-image', exact=True) }}
       - numpydoc
       - sphinx
       - something else
  - name: scikit-image.all
    requirements:
       run:
       - {{ pin_subpackage('scikit-image', exact=True) }}
       - {{ pin_subpackage('scikit-image.test', exact=True) }}
       - {{ pin_subpackage('scikit-image.doc', exact=True) }}
```

  * People are understandably scared to break dependencies, so making `pack`, mean a minimum set of requirements is unlikely to fly (although I would wholly support it). That said, people seemed quite open to creating split packages that would pull in additional dependencies.

That said, we would probably need a good **naming convention**. The square brackets of pip are kinda nice, but maybe something like a `.` might do for conda? Dashes are being proposed for things like *build variants* which might mean something compiled with different options and be mutually exclusive as opposed to additional dependencies which are additive. I would much rather have dashes indicate the use of more recent instruction sets :whale:

@jakirkham @msarahan  @jjhelmus any ideas on a good naming convention for optional dependencies that won't break things.

I recognize that symlinks are valuable, but in my view, their fragility demands that we offer them only to users to assent to their risks. I'm not saying we should get rid of them, only that users or the sysadmin explicitly ask for them. 

Regardless, your suggested change is a good one.

I do think that, as part of our overall goals to fully support centrally administered installations, we could eliminate the fragility issue. For instance, when a package is sym-linked for the first time, we could mark its package cache directory as "locked" so that it cannot be accidentally removed. We could implement a conservative reference counting scheme that counts the number of environments that have symlinked to that package, and allow it to be removed only when the count is zero.

FWIW, to run Linux conda on docker on macOS (I assume that's what you are doing here?), I created a case-sensitive volume mounted at `/opt` into which I install all my `conda` Linux aoftware. With APFS this was easy. You should also create an entry in `/etc/fstab` such as:

```
UUID=<FILE_SYSTEM_UUID> /opt apfs rw 0 2
```
There should be a simpler example that would do the same thing. This might be a place to start ( https://github.com/jakirkham/test_conda_features/ ).

For anyone still coming across this thread needing the solution...

You need to back up to your previous version of conda, and then you can update to conda latest (right now 4.0.4).

The output of

    ls $(/conda/bin/conda info | grep cache | cut -d: -f2) | grep -E "^conda-\d"

should show you the version numbers available in your cache. Then from there, for example if 3.19.3 was the version number indicated

    conda install conda=3.19.3

You can also just install miniconda over the top of your current installation, and then also upgrade from there.  You can download the latest miniconda installer from this page: http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html

I don't understand why it isn't.  How is tmux moving across *some* environment variables into the subprocess, but not *all* environment variables?  It seems to me like tmux is maybe a bit disrespectful or arrogant with setting `PATH` to something that's hard-coded without inheritance.  Something like

    export PATH="/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin"

rather than

    export PATH="/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin:$PATH"

Conda, and I'm sure a lot of other software for that matter, uses coupled state among environment variables.  tmux is totally breaking that it seems.

So the reason why the installation of extra packages at build time is problematic for us is that the package in question is a compiler. Cling requires patched versions of clang and llvm at runtime.

The only package tracking the cling feature is the `cling-patches` empty package so we are following best practices on that front.

Now another requirement of the cling interpreter is that we have very strict requirements in terms of binary compatibility. For example the interpreted c++ must use the same STL that was used to *build* clang. So on linux it would be that of gcc-7, which we ship as a conda package on our channel. For similar reasons we need our own builds of other dependencies such as cryptopp, zeromq, which must also be built with gcc-7 on linux, or whichever host compiler we decide for on other platforms. We have been using build features to make sure that our version was picked up, versus the same package on default or conda forge.

Now to build these things, we must be careful about the version of clang used to build things (the conda package or the host version). The ultimate solution in the future for OS X will be to build clang with itself to prevent this mismatch between the host compiler and the version of clang used at runtime.

@mizeng if you are using Curator as it ships, it already does [automatically exclude all standard Kibana index types](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/cli/index_selection.py#L106-L120):

```python
    if ctx.parent.info_name == "delete": # Protect against accidental delete
        logger.info("Pruning Kibana-related indices to prevent accidental deletion.")
        working_list = prune_kibana(working_list)

    # If there are manually added indices, we will add them here
    working_list.extend(in_list(index, indices))

    if working_list and ctx.parent.info_name == 'delete':
        # If filter by disk space, filter the working_list by space:
        if ctx.parent.params['disk_space']:
            logger.info("Filtering to keep disk usage below {0} gigabytes".format(ctx.parent.params['disk_space']))
            working_list = filter_by_space(
                                client, working_list,
                                disk_space=ctx.parent.params['disk_space'],
                                reverse=ctx.parent.params['reverse']
                           )
```

The [`prune_kibana` method](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/api/utils.py#L273-L286) is used to remove these indices from the working list before any action is taken.

If you are using the Curator API, you'd be correct.  `filter_by_space` does not, by itself, automatically prune Kibana indices.  It should be simple to add the `prune_kibana` method before or after running the `filter_by_space` method.

**NOTE:** If you are using an older version of Curator, the `filter_by_space` method was called differently and did not act as a special filter, but was called `delete_by_space`.  With one of these older versions, it may have been possible for Curator to have deleted a Kibana index in a delete-by-space operation.  You will automatically have this protection if you upgrade to the most recent version of Curator.

I got this to work.

I noticed that `openssl` was built on gcc 4.3, and python was built on 4.8 and that seemed as though it should work, however it was worth looking into.

This is what i did to fix the issue:

- I recompiled a new python (python 3.6) with the same version of `gcc` that was used for `python-devel` and `openssh-devel`.  I did this with a `make altinstall` like so (because i don't have root):

```` bash
me@my-host:~/joes_python/src/Python-3.6.1> pwd
/home/me/joes_python/src/Python-3.6.1
me@my-host:~/joes_python/src/Python-3.6.1> ./configure && make altinstall prefix=/home/me/joes_python exec-prefix=/home/me/joes_python  
```` 

Once this finished I had newly compiled python3.6 binaries in `~/joes_python/bin`.

----

In order to see if `azure-cli` worked with the newly compiled python, i did the following:

1. create a `virtualenv` pointing to my newly created python:
````
me@my-host:~/joes_az> pwd
/home/me/joes_az
me@my-host:~/joes_az> virtualenv -p /home/me/joes_python/bin/python3.6 ~/joes_az/venv
```` 
2. activate this venv:
````
me@my-host:~/joes_az> source venv/bin/activate
(venv) me@my-host:~/joes_az> 
````
3. pip install `azure-cli`:
```` bash
(venv) me@my-host:~/joes_az> python --version ; pip --version
Python 3.6.1
pip 9.0.1 from /home/me/joes_az/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages (python 3.6)
(venv) me@my-host:~/joes_az> 
(venv) me@my-host:~/joes_az> pip install azure-cli 
#.... clip .... 
````
4. After that, i have a new binary with for `az` in the path below, and i can see login works:
```` bash
(venv) me@my-host:~/joes_az> ./venv/bin/az login 
To sign in, use a web browser to open the page https://the-web-site/loginpage and enter the code *******  to authenticate.

````

----

So, the issue didn't have anything to do with suse.  With this info i should be able to help my sys admin clean up the system and get me a working install.  Thanks for your help,

It would be nice if PyBullet got some proper publicity indeed.

> The modification to the MJCF importer also affects PyBullet though.

I prefer to maintain backward compatibility, and not starting to read new fields that were ignored before. So we would need to add flags like URDF_USE_INERTIA_FROM_FILE for MJCF, and URDF_USE_DAMPING_FROM_FILE etc. It seems easier to just add a line in pybullet for the linear/angular damping.

Note that the current linear/angular damping for btMultiBody is not joint damping: the velocity of each body and link is dampened. So in the case of a body that consists only of a base and no child links/joints, there is still damping.

The body linear/angular velocity damping is implemented here:
~~~~
for the base:
https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/src/BulletDynamics/Featherstone/btMultiBody.cpp#L826
and for child links:
https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/src/BulletDynamics/Featherstone/btMultiBody.cpp#L913
~~~~
This body damping can only be changed using pybullet.changeDynamics.

Separately, there is some explicit joint damping applied here:
https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/examples/SharedMemory/PhysicsServerCommandProcessor.cpp#L5868

This joint damping is read from the URDF file here:
https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/8c04bc9473b4c179d9781fc3577910354a2d9d84/examples/Importers/ImportURDFDemo/UrdfParser.cpp#L1095

quotes = quotes_historical_yahoo("INTC", date1, date2)
if len(quotes) == 0:
    raise SystemExit
 
# unpack quotes
dates = np.array([q[0] for q in quotes], dtype=int)
close_v = np.array([q[2] for q in quotes])
volume = np.array([q[5] for q in quotes])[1:]
 
# take diff of close value
# this makes len(diff) = len(close_t) - 1
# therefore, others quantity also need to be shifted
diff = close_v[1:] - close_v[:-1]
dates = dates[1:]
close_v = close_v[1:]
 
# pack diff and volume for training
X = np.column_stack([diff, volume])
 
###############################################################################
# Run Gaussian HMM
print("fitting to HMM and decoding ...", end='')
n_components = 5
 
# make an HMM instance and execute fit
model = GaussianHMM(n_components, covariance_type="diag", n_iter=1000)
 
model.fit([X])
 
# predict the optimal sequence of internal hidden state
hidden_states = model.predict(X)
 
print("done\n")
 
###############################################################################
# print trained parameters and plot
print("Transition matrix")
print(model.transmat_)
print()
 
print("means and vars of each hidden state")
for i in range(n_components):
    print("%dth hidden state" % i)
    print("mean = ", model.means_[i])
    print("var = ", np.diag(model.covars_[i]))
    print()
 
years = YearLocator()   # every year
months = MonthLocator()  # every month
yearsFmt = DateFormatter('%Y')
fig = pl.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111)
 
for i in range(n_components):
    # use fancy indexing to plot data in each state
    idx = (hidden_states == i)
    ax.plot_date(dates[idx], close_v[idx], 'o', label="%dth hidden state" % i)
ax.legend()
 
# format the ticks
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(years)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(yearsFmt)
ax.xaxis.set_minor_locator(months)
ax.autoscale_view()
 
# format the coords message box
ax.fmt_xdata = DateFormatter('%Y-%m-%d')
ax.fmt_ydata = lambda x: '$%1.2f' % x
ax.grid(True)
 
fig.autofmt_xdate()
pl.show()

With nbextensions 4.x, there isn't a really reliable way of doing what you
are asking. This is mainly because nbextensions are not versioned in any
way.

With the new npm based 5.x approach, code in the kernel or server will be
able to specify which version of an npm package they require. A user might
have 5 different versions of that package installed, but we (and npm) will
make sure that only the needed one gets loaded. This allows you to have
separate installation of the Python and JS side, while still making sure
versions match.

So, I've picked up this work where @aggFTW left off.  I think this is how we're thinking about doing this:

1. Use a custom SessionManager that passes down the notebook name as an argument to the MappingKernelManager.  

2. Use a custom KernelManager that communicates the notebook name to the new kernel process on startup (through an environment variable or some other method).  

3. Our custom kernels will take the notebook name as a key and will update their metadata as appropriate in the way that @aggFTW described above.

4. Use a custom ContentsManager to update the metadata necessary for resuming stale sessions when a method is renamed.

Item (4) will certainly be an internal extension to Jupyter for us, but we were wondering whether items (1) and (2) would have any chance of being accepted upstream.  I understand that the kernel not knowing what's talking to it is part of the design, but it seems like it would be generally useful (not just for this scenario) if kernels could be made aware what the name of their notebook is either through an environment variable, a command-line argument, or a 0mq message.  Do you suppose there would be any interest in that PR?

In the v2.0 development version, `import_data` takes a `recursive` boolean option. When `True`, the data in submodels will also be merged. When `False` (the default), it works like `dict.update()`.

Creating an instance does not automatically run validation. You're hitting a *conversion error* at the import stage, which is distinct from a validation error.

Every time you import data, you should wrap the code in a try–except construct to catch any conversion errors. Or you could override `Model.convert()` and turn the errors into warnings there.

To say a bit more, autograd works by wrapping numpy's functions so that when evaluating a gradient they do some extra bookkeeping on the side. That's why you need to replace imports like `import numpy as np` with `import autograd.numpy as np`.

It's an easy mistake to make (I did the same in #49!) and the error message is pretty opaque. I just added a hint to the error message, so that when running your test case above this prints out at the bottom of the traceback:

```
AutogradHint: This error *might* be caused by importing numpy instead of autograd.numpy, so check that you have 'import autograd.numpy as np' at the top of your file instead of 'import numpy as np'.
Sub-exception:
TypeError: make_grad_np_sum() got an unexpected keyword argument 'dtype'
```

I like the suggestions in your most recent comment (wow, has it already been 25 days?). I think it is important to have `check_grads` accessible from the autograd package, since it's such a handy function when writing custom VJPs or even when convincing yourself that autograd is doing the right thing on your code. However, I'm not sure how to make an alias to tests/numpy_utils.py:check_grads without also including the tests directory in the autograd package. 

Maybe we can have an alias of `check_grads` in numpy_utils.py somehow, with the definition still in the autograd package?

...you are correct.  It definitely should (and is) generating an exception.  To catch a custom / package exception, you have to import that exception into your namespace.  You are just missing ``SparkApiError`` from your ``from ciscosparkapi import ...`` statement.

Your code should look like this:
``` python
#!/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/bin/python3
# coding=utf-8
import os
from ciscosparkapi import CiscoSparkAPI, SparkApiError

os.environ['SPARK_ACCESS_TOKEN'] = 'xyzxyz'
api = CiscoSparkAPI()
try:
    message = api.messages.create('123098123098123098123098123', text='message_text')
    print("New message created, with ID:", message.id)
    print(message.text)
except SparkApiError as e:
    print(e)
```

Which runs and does indeed catch the exception(s) and print them when they are fired.

The loop can be shared with other tasks, not only Telethon. So opening and closing the loop is not under control of Telethon.

My general usecase is the following:
```
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
main = asyncio.ensure_future(main(loop), loop=loop)
for signame in ('SIGINT', 'SIGTERM'):
    loop.add_signal_handler(getattr(signal, signame), loop.stop)
loop.run_forever()  # at this point you are waiting the loop stops
main.cancel()
loop.run_until_complete(main)
pending = asyncio.Task.all_tasks(loop)
for task in pending:
    task.cancel()
loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*pending, loop=loop))
loop.close()
```

Right now probably `_recv_loop_impl` is not ready for `asyncio.CancelledError` but it will done.

I'm not sure I understand your problem. You want to cluster all the markers together? Then how about putting all your markers in a single  `MarkerCluster`? Also, you don't need a `FeatureGroup` when you're already using a `MarkerCluster`. For example:

```
import folium.plugins

m = folium.Map(location=[0, 0], zoom_start=2)
lats = [37.25715519, -31.72884313, -75.27080391, 50.30897559]
lons = [167.20482296, 23.5310324, 164.47413453, 26.87409742]

cluster = folium.plugins.MarkerCluster().add_to(m)
for i in range(0, 4):
    folium.Marker(location=[lats[i], lons[i]], popup=str(i)).add_to(cluster)

folium.LayerControl(collapsed=False).add_to(m)
m.save('map.html')
```

About colors: the `MarkerCluster` has customization options for the icons, the popups and the cluster icons. Check out the examples in the example gallery:
http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/python-visualization/folium/blob/master/examples/MarkerCluster.ipynb

@frol When using `Meta.fields` with vanilla marshmallow, the fields are created implicitly upon invoking `dump` or `load`, at which point marshmallow infers which field types to use based upon the objects being dumped. Prior to invoking `dump`/`load`, apispec cannot inspect and translate those fields to swagger.

ModelSchemas infer and create fields upon class declaration, so apispec is able to translate those fields.

`@validator` is currently used to define schema-level validation. I don't think it makes sense to conflate its usage with field-level validation. For one, the parameters for `@validator` (`raw` and `pass_original`) only make sense in the context of schema-level validation. Therefore, if the above proposal does get implemented, it would probably be as a separate decorator.

There may be use cases where a user might want to validate the values in a Dict field, in which case we could adapt from DRF's `DictField`,

```python
various_data = fields.Dict(fields.Str())

If you don't pass an inner field, there would be no additional validation of values.

Ok, I think I see what you are after. Newly created objects are in a `Transient` state whereas newly loaded objects are in `Pending` state.

The reason that is is currently the case is that it is possible to do this:

```
author = Author(name='Chuck Paluhniuk')
book = Book(title='Fight Club', author=author)
session.add(author)
session.add(book)
session.commit()
print author
# <Author(name=u'Chuck Paluhniuk')>

dump_data = {'id': 1}
author = author_schema.load(dump_data, session=session).data
print author
# <Author(name=u'Chuck Paluhniuk')>
```

This is a pretty typical use-case so you can see why it is done that way.

I assume what you are trying to do is load new data from serialized format, but have some criteria where you want to add them to the session or not. I assume you also want to have all the objects around for reasons other than committing? Or perhaps you want to add them to different sessions (although you could do that one use case with the current implementation) or at a time of your choosing etc.
```
dump_data = {'books': [1], 'name': 'Chuck Paluhniuk'}
author = author_schema.load(dump_data, session=session).data
# Some arbitrary criteria
if author.books:
    session.add(author)
# Make a list of all authors
all_authors.append(author)

# Do something with all the authors regardless of whether they were added to session
print authors
```

All right, changed the implementation to store `inv_std` instead of `var`. This makes the docstring and the code a bit more ugly. I've written the code such that it can still use `T.nnet.batch_normalization()` during training, supplying `mean`, `std`, `gamma` and `beta` as before. During testing, it also uses `T.nnet.batch_normalization()`, setting `std=1` and `gamma=gamma*inv_std`. This way it can still benefit from the memory savings in `mode='low_mem'`, and perform like the equivalent vanilla Theano code in `mode='high_mem'`. This is all done in good faith that `T.nnet.batch_normalization()` provides a benefit even if one of its argument is set to a scalar `1`.

The main benefit of the added ugliness is that we could add a `DNNBatchNormLayer` later, with identical parameterization. If we stick to the previous implementation, `DNNBatchNormLayer` would have a different parameter from `BatchNormLayer` (i.e., `inv_std` instead of `var`), but it would be possible to convert between the two. I currently don't expect that Theano will provide a generic batch normalization Op that could be used by `BatchNormLayer` to seamlessly support CPU, GPU and cuDNN -- this would be a much stronger argument for using `inv_std` instead of `var`. And I currently don't expect that computing the running average of `inv_std` is better than the running average of `var` -- again, this would be a much stronger argument for making the code more ugly.

Conclusion: I'm not very happy about the ugly code, and tend to think that different interfaces for a cuDNN- and non-cuDNN version wouldn't be so bad after all...

Ok, I'll fix things up and more (code) explanations and tests. The name `plumb` was just temporary.

The basic conceptual idea is that when you call `io.write`, it writes all the data for normal data source. For iterator data sources it also writes all the data currently accumulated in the iterator. This is how it currently works and I didn't change it. Basically in `io.write` it does something like:

```py
for channel in channels:
    for data in channel.data:
        do something
```
The problem with this is that when you call `io.write`, it iterates through the `channel.data` iterator until it's exhausted and the function returns, at which point the iterator is dead and further calls to it will just keep raising `StopIteration` and not return new data. The problem is that I would like to keep adding data to the iterator as it's being generated and then and keep calling `write` say every 5 minutes to save, which is impossible once the iterator is exhausted. I also don't want to block in `write` of course to wait for the new data.

Instead, I created my `DataChunkStream` class, where if you look, it's `__iter__` method creates a new generator/iterator each time it's called. That means we can call the above code `for data in channel.data` many times until the current iterator it's exhausted and it raises a `StopIteration`, meaning all the data accumulated until now has been written. Now, as new data is generated and added to `DataChunkStream`, we'll call `plumb_data` each time, and each time it does `for data in channel.data` it'll generate a new iterator which will iterate over the existing data until done.

So basically, I can call `io.write` once to initialize everything and write the not iterable data. Then, I can call `plumb_data` periodically, which goes over all the channels that have iterator data and write them. This would only work of course for iterators which generate a new iterator every time it's called, hence why I added the `DataChunkStream` class. I realize I may not have been very clear about this, and maybe that's what @ajtritt was asking? Do you still think I should remove it? This kind of iterable class can be generated by users, but I included it because I figured it'd be more convenient as not all users would fully understand how to do that. It's also pretty easy to use as you can just append to the queue.

IMO the only connection API we should supply is ``vehicle.last_heartbeat``. This should have a value of ``None`` before a connection is established and we're getting heartbeats. After that it should be time since we got the last heartbeat (or possibly time since we got "message of any kind" from the vehicle). 

My reasoning is that any other information implies that we really have a connection (ie are receiving and sending messages). In fact the only thing we KNOW is the freshness of the last message. I want this in people's minds. I don't see adding a boolean they can check on as making this any simpler than just defining a callback on the time value.

We will still need something to perform the function of ``vehicle.connected`` but it really needs to imply "valid vehicle" rather than connection state (ie solely for ``vehicle.close()`` and as a backup if the user does not connection monitoring.. I would also propose that we use it to (after removing any callbacks) cause other function calls on the object to raise an exception . That will encourage the developer to immediately set the value to ``None`` themselves (a familiar pattern for those who learned C++ first :-0 ). If we do it this way probably no need to have an API to set this timeout time.

Irrespective of whether we have the additional boolean for tracking a timeout we still need to think about what advice we give people for how they take action on it/set the value.

Any ideas?

My thoughts ...

> The  ``vehicle.last_heartbeat`` value is a measure of the "freshness" of information from the vehicle. Scripts which rely on timely position information may wish to modify their behaviour based on this value (for example when tracking a moving target).

> Higher values of this attribute (>5 seconds, say) may indicate that the link is broken (in which case developers may wish to pause or restart the script). 

> Note: The connection to the vehicle is potentially "lossy": Irrespective of the last_heartbeat value you cannot assume that messages are (or are not) getting to the vehicle.

... then having an example that perhaps just exits the script following a 6 second timeout?

```python
>>> config['Configuration'].RUNTIME_PATH = 'Dirac'
>>> config['Configuration'].RUNTIME_PATH
'Dirac:'
>>> config['Configuration'].RUNTIME_PATH = 'LHCb'
>>> config['Configuration'].RUNTIME_PATH
'LHCb:Dirac:'
>>> config['Configuration'].RUNTIME_PATH = 'Dirac'
>>> config['Configuration'].RUNTIME_PATH

Let's be careful to keep this discussion in perspective. We want the same things. Everyone involved wants code to be legible. Nobody is arguing in favor of complexity or bad implementations.

But these are subjective measurements whose long-term value is difficult to quantify, and that is the reason for the proposed arrangement.

As you invest more time and energy in moving those measurements in a positive direction, the incremental return on your investment diminishes. Different teams and individuals have to make their own conclusions about how far to go, and at what point the value obtained no longer justifies the expense. The differing needs and circumstances of each project will naturally lead to different conclusions.

The smash project wants to invest more heavily than the pulp project does. In my experience, both projects have a desired level of investment that is higher than what we find in the general python OSS community. That's fine and admirable, and everyone can support smash's desired standards. The proposed agreement allows external contributors to submit PRs that meet a reasonable and generally agreeable set of standards that are likely to be compatible with most contributors' expectations, while allowing smash maintainers to provide extra lift at the end to bring PRs up to smash's higher-than-average standards. The pulp team has also done this with many community contributions, and we've found it to be a successful arrangement.

The concern from @asmacdo is that as an external contributor who also has been asked to help with PR review, he does not want to be responsible for providing that tail-end lift. I think that's very reasonable, and is likely agreeable by all. External reviewers should focus on areas where the author will be asked to make changes.

I confirm that the middleware's `resolve()` methods get called in reverse order from how they're listed when passed into `schema.execute`. Let's say your middleware is:
```
class CharPrintingMiddleware(object):
        def __init__(self, char):
            self.char = char

        def resolve(self, next, *args, **kwargs):
            print(f'resolve() called for middleware {self.char}')
            return next(*args, **kwargs).then(
                lambda x: print(f'then() for {self.char}')
            )
```
and you call:
```
schema.execute(
    statement, 
    middleware=[
        CharAppendingMiddleware('a'), 
        CharAppendingMiddleware('b'), 
        CharAppendingMiddleware('c')
    ]
)
```
Then the printed output is:
```
resolve() called for middleware c
resolve() called for middleware b
resolve() called for middleware a
then() for a
then() for b
then() for c
```

This makes me wonder if perhaps it was intentional to have middleware called in reverse order, and the intention was that `then()` set up by the middleware would wind up being called in order.

How is it not secure or trusted? If you configure the following:

    IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER = ('REMOTE_ADDR', )

You are good to go if you are not running behind a reverse proxy. `ipware` should not check for additional headers, and you will get the value for `REMOTE_ADDR` as your end user IP.

If you do have a reverse proxy, for example `nginx`, that *sets* the client header instead of appending to a header value to a chain of headers provided by the client making the request, you are also good to go:

    # nginx configuration for location with http_realip_module enabled
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;

    # Django project configuration for ipware
    IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER = ('HTTP_X_REAL_IP', 'REMOTE_ADDR', )

The cases where `django-ipware` becomes unsecure is

1. when you are using the default `ipware` `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` headers (or `request.META` values to be exact) to check the client IP and you allow a client to set and your proxy to forward those headers arbitrarily (which is how many proxy servers work out-of-the-box), or
2. you check against an appended header value such as `X-Forwarded-For` or `request.META['HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR']` in Django terms, for which we can not currently configure the `proxy_count` as used by `ipware` at this moment. We can add a configuration flag for this to allow for accounting of proxy count as well.

https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware/blob/a71d956c1c3c21c80e742823687f39e970e1147c/ipware/ip2.py#L5

The things that can happen to your project because of this are

1. someone is able to make multiple authentication requests from a single IP by spoofing headers, bypassing `axes` lock out mechanisms, which any serious attacker apart from a script kiddy with a single computer will indeed do if they are really interested in breaching your site by e.g. using a fleet of low cost spot pricing cloud servers that have changing IPs, or
2. you lock out users by accident because you use e.g. the `REMOTE_ADDR` attribute behind a proxy which gets set to `127.0.0.1` and you end up seeing multiple authentication requests coming from the same address, locking that address, and all your users that seem to use the same address, out.

The case that is really potentially insecure here is case 1, where you allow your clients to effectively inject semantical headers that you use for secure checking into your service, and do not check their semantics or counts. Even after bypassing the lock out mechanisms your attacker still has to brute-force their way in, usually requiring and unrealistic amount of attempts over a slow HTTP connection over TCP/IP socket as an attack vector and a brute-force attack against (most probably) PBKDF2 hashing, which is rather slow as default. Then your attacker has to actually gain access to a meaningful account to exploit your service, meaning that you have to have a service that requires heightened security running with non-MFA staff users. After those steps, yes, an attacker can gain access to your accounts, and if you use single-factor authentication as in just a password to actually log in as a staff user in a heightened security online service, I'd say you are actually fucked by design.

Or is there something I'm missing here? Is there an easier and simpler attack vector that is open, if you have configured your proxy, `ipware`, and `axes` correctly?

I think I tracked this down. 8cb21aabbcba6aac389b2c073024fea08878126e notes:

> Django starting with 1.9 switched to using a class to provide an
iterator for the querymanager.  Between 1.9 and 1.10 changes slowly
stopped referencing that function and instead started calling
_iterator_class directly.

This may be true for Django 1.11, but on Django 1.9 and 1.10 ``QuerySet.iterator`` [is still called from](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.7/django/db/models/query.py#L1087) ``QuerySet._fetch_all``:

    def _fetch_all(self):
        if self._result_cache is None:
            self._result_cache = list(self.iterator())
        if self._prefetch_related_lookups and not self._prefetch_done:
            self._prefetch_related_objects()

So, on Django 1.9 and 1.10, we have the following call chain: ``QuerySet._fetch_all`` → ``InheritanceQuerySetMixin.iterator()`` → ``QuerySet.iterator()`` → ``QuerySet._iterator_class()`` (that is ``InheritanceIterable``) returning back to ``InheritanceQuerySetMixin.iterator()``. Because the iteration logic was copied, we then [run all the ``_get_sub_obj_recurse`` logic again](https://github.com/jazzband/django-model-utils/blob/3.0.0/model_utils/managers.py#L110-L134), but on the selected subclass instances. Now, because the cached values from the `select_related(...)` call in `select_subclasses()` get lost when selecting the subclass instances, the database is hit again, _for ~ each item in the queryset!_

Feedback discussed offline.

I'm only loosely describing how I imagine MySQL works, based on my interpretation of the docs. I haven't examined the implementation or tested it, and there are many other databases out there we hope to support. I do however strongly suspect the Tensors / BigTensors dichotomy will protect us from what we don't know, and I'm looking forward to testing this design in the near future, once we get this up and running.

Tinier rows should make scanning queries like COUNT go faster---even if the DB implementation stores ->next pointers at the beginning of each row, to the next row, because each of those skips could result in a disk seek, and would cause us to lose the benefits of OS FS caching readahead. It's also rare, IIRC, for databases to implement a COUNT counter for each table, since it creates a hotspot for disk contention, and wouldn't help for COUNT queries with a predicate. I also haven't tested this, but I'm pretty sure it's true, because I feel like the fact that these databases were designed to force rows to be as small as possible serves as evidence that other engineers determined it to be true.

I made a couple slight adjustments to the code, like filling paragraphs, and documenting some string limits. The run name can now be up to 1900 characters since people like to put hyperparameters in there. I didn't set it higher to avoid encouraging the storage of rows greater than half a 4kB page. Since it needs two bytes for the length regardless, we can increase it further in the future without a migration.

The thing with Pillow is that it depends on a couple of native Linux libraries like `jpeg`. Users become a little confused because

1. They might not know TensorBuilder uses `tflearn`, although it is mentioned somewhere.
2. Its even less probable that they know what `pillow` is.

So they don't understand the error and its generally a bad experience when just doing an import statement.

BTW:  TensorBuilder's `core` literally is just a layer that enables the use of nice syntax, to the point I actually don't code the operations that deal with Tensors myself, I register functions from various libraries at runtime (I do preserve the original documentation and clarify what module it comes from). I've done it in such a way that, if you so desired, you could **patch** an untainted `Builder` class with only TFLearn functions and you get the DSL for free. My approach for TensorBuilder as a useful library is to give a curated patch with useful functions from any library, I might make the core a separate library to make the separation more clear. I think I'll be sponsored to improve the library.

```
alias aws-cevo-demo='unset AWS_PROFILE; aws-google-auth -k -p cevo-demo; export AWS_PROFILE=cevo-demo'
alias aws-cevo-dev='unset AWS_PROFILE; aws-google-auth -k -p cevo-dev; export AWS_PROFILE=cevo-dev'
```

This way auth is always launched from the default profile, and then once `aws-google-auth` has finished, it is set to the newly auth'd profile.

I suspect what's happening here is that both A and B are being destroyed at the same time ("same" being within a single garbage collection).  Essentially keep_alive is a bit weaker than you're expecting: it really only guarantees that A won't be admitted for garbage collection before B, but doesn't guarantee that they can't be admitted together (and as far as I know Python doesn't provide any guarantees on the order of destruction of objects within a garbage collection pass).  If they are admitted together, you can get out-of-order destruction, as you're seeing.

I'm not sure that there's a simple workaround that could be done to deal with this on the pybind11 side (especially since neither object actually has to be pybind-managed).

You ought to be able to work around it by storing by storing a `shared_ptr<B>` instead of a `B &` (and making `B`'s holder `shared_ptr<B>`); that should let the C++ object live beyond the destruction of the wrapping Python object.

Exactly that. The new-style implementation of `void_t` is `template<class...> using void_t = void;`, i.e. no need for a helper `struct`. The new libc++ implements it that way, but clang 4.0 doesn't implement [CWG1558](http://open-std.org/JTC1/SC22/WG21/docs/cwg_defects.html#1558) so it just ignores unused alias template arguments. The interesting thing is that this did work before, sometime in clang 3.x.

Anyway, I just removed `__cpp_lib_void_t` rather than adding extra compiler version checks since both gcc and clang are in `c++1z` mode for now.

```
python2.7 python27 python2 python
```

If one of those binaries corresponds with Python 2.7 and appears in the list before the Python 2.6 binary, deleting the virtual environment with `rm -rf ${XDG_DATA_HOME:-~/.local/share}/letsencrypt` and rerunning `certbot-auto` should install the client using Python 2.7.

If you do want pywebpush to autofill the 'aud', you need to make sure it is
not specified and not just blank.

e.g. {"sub": "foo@example.com"} and not {"aud":"", "sub":"foo@example.com"}

Google's errors aren't terribly informative. An Unauthorized error could be
due to a missing VAPID field, or using the incorrect private key, or any
number of other reasons. Make sure that you have a proper VAPID key pair,
and that you use the PUBLIC key when registering and the PRIVATE key when
signing the VAPID header. The VAPID key should NOT be the same key you're
using to encrypt the body of the message, but I'm guessing you're not doing
that if you're calling my library.

yes, exactly

It is open because current implementation (v1.2.2) has extension `.dos` for LET data cube file. See:
https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/v1.2.2/pytrip/let.py#L38

```
class LETCube(Cube):
(...)
    data_file_extension = "dos"
```

This also means that currently `parse_path` method of Cube class: https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/master/pytrip/cube.py#L499
thinks that file with `.dos` extension can be either dose cube or let cube.

Also right now all header files have univeral extension `.hed`

We also assume currently that `filename = core_name + extension` and when reading a cube we need two files:
 - `corename +'.hed'`
 - `corename + data_file_extension`

This means we can read for example pair of file `1.hed` + `1.ctx`, but we do not support reading `2.hed` + `1.ctx`. Both files currently needs to have same corename.

To fix this issue following things has to implemented:
 - change data_file_extension for LETCube to `dosemlet.dos`
 - introduce header extension specific to a data class: `dosemlet.hed` for LETCube and `.hed` for all other classes

The first version would have lost some of the efficiencies. Would something like this be suitable?
```python
class PolymorphicModelIterable(ModelIterable):
    def __iter__(self):
        base_result_objects = super(PolymorphicModelIterable, self).__iter__()
        real_instances = self.queryset._get_real_instances(base_result_objects)
        for obj in real_instances:
            yield obj
```
IIUC, the first version is more efficient if only a small portion of the results is retrieved, because the code would only fetch objects from the database on demand, versus fetching everything and keeping the results in a cache (`real_instances`) in the second version. However, the second version should drastically reduce the number of queries when most models are from a subclass.

The current behavior (duplicating everything) is intentional in order to provide more isolation.

A natural way to do what you want is to allow an optional `--redis-address` to be passed in to your script. If it isn't passed in, then it calls `ray.init()`. If it is passed in then it passes the redis address into `ray.init`. For example, see

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/b020e6bf1fb00d0745371d8674146d4a5b75d9f0/examples/hyperopt/hyperopt_simple.py#L19-L26

To make simplify the process of figuring out the address, you could call `ray start` with flags like `--node-ip-address=127.0.0.1 --redis-port=6379`, and then you should be able to just use `--redis-address=127.0.0.1:6379`. Though that IP address will only work if you're using Ray on a single machine.

In terms of automatically discovering an existing Ray cluster. In the single machine case, we could just check if there's a redis server on port 6379. In the multi machine case we may need to integrate something like etcd for service discovery.

For anyone who may come after me. **I am not sure if all steps are required**, but they helped me narrow down the possible problem sources. **Pretty sure only step 7** as above. So, perhaps just moving files to backups is a more appropriate strategy and slowly adding them back to their proper paths, e.g. `mv ~/.jupyter ~/.jupyter.bak` is better than `rm ~/.jupyter`

1. Uninstalled _jupyter-contrib-nbextensions_, _ipywidgets_, _ipython_genutils_, from the target conda environment. Uninstalled _ipywidgets_ from everywhere, system, user, and sys-prefix.

2. Removed the _jupyter_contrib_nbextensions_ config files ( `common.json, edit.json, tree.json, terminal.json, notebook.json, jupyter_nvconver_config.json`) from `/usr/local/share/jupyter/nbextensions` and `/usr/local/share/jupyter/nbextensions`. Not sure the sub-directories, but a simple `find /usr -name "*.json"` should reveal them.

3. Removed my `~/.jupyter` user config files in order to force use of the conda environment config files. Also made sure to target any extensions from the packages in *1* located in `~/.local/share/jupyter/nbextensions`. I don't have a `~/.ipython` folder anymore, pretty sure that's depricated, so that went too.

4. Manually **edited** (because I have other extensions I wanted to keep) the conda env config files (same as in step 2) in `~/anaconda3/envs/myenv/etc/jupyter/nbconfig` to delete any reference to any of the _jupyter-contrib-nbextensions_ or _ipywidgets_ extensions, whether enabled or not. Watch out for trailing commas at the end of the `json` lists!

5. Reinstall all the things! `conda install -c conda-forge jupyter_contrib_nbextensions ipywidgets`

6. Enable _jupyter_contrib_nbextensions_ things with `jupyter contrib nbextension install --sys-prefix` and `jupyter nbextensions_configurator install --sys-prefix` (shouldn't be needed, but doesn't hurt)

7. Enable _ipywidgets_ as above with `jupyter nbextension enable --py --sys-prefix widgetsnbextension`. Pretty sure this was the magic step.

Here's an example with a checkbox:

```python
import ipywidgets as widgets

def initial_condition(): 
    label_iniCond = widgets.HTML("""The Initial elevation file dictates the 
         dry/wet zones of the bathymetry. The initial U and V velocity files specifies 
        the velocities in the x and y directions. They must be uploaded in 
        the Jupyter Notebook Home folder before running the simulation.""",
        layout = widgets.Layout(height = '45px', width = '90%',
                    size = '20'))

    space_box = widgets.Box(layout=widgets.Layout(height ='25px', width='90%')) 
    # this ^ box is created to have space among widgets 

    # initial elevation widget container (label and textbox)
    label_iniElev = widgets.Label('Initial Elevation Text File:',
    layout =  widgets.Layout(width = '18%'))
    iniElev_text = widgets.Text(layout=widgets.Layout(width = "50%",height = '85px'))
    container_iniElev = widgets.HBox(children=[label_iniElev,iniElev_text])

    # initial u vel widget container (label and textbox)
    label_Uvel = widgets.Label('Initial U Velocity Text File:', 
    layout = widgets.Layout(width = '18%'))
    Uvel_text = widgets.Text(layout = iniElev_text.layout)
    container_Uvel = widgets.HBox(children=[label_Uvel,Uvel_text])

    # initial v vel widget container (label and textbox)
    label_Vvel = widgets.Label('Initial V Velocity Text File:', 
    layout = widgets.Layout(width = '18%'))
    Vvel_text = widgets.Text(layout = iniElev_text.layout)
    container_Vvel = widgets.HBox(children=[label_Vvel,Vvel_text])

    iniCond_box = widgets.VBox(children=[label_iniCond,space_box,container_iniElev,
                                container_Uvel,container_Vvel])
    return iniCond_box


init = initial_condition()
show_initial = Checkbox(description='Show initial conditions')

def toggle_initial(change):
    if change['new']:
        #show
        init.layout.display=''
    else:
        # hide
        init.layout.display='none'

show_initial.observe(toggle_initial, 'value')
toggle_initial({'new': show_initial.value})
VBox([show_initial, init])
```

[global]

```

You'll have to provide your own configuration file on the command line:

```bash
docker run -it -v /path/to/my/configuration/of/python-gitlab.cfg:/python-gitlab.cfg python-gitlab <my command>
```

Which could lead to:

```bash
docker run -it -v /path/to/my/configuration/of/python-gitlab.cfg:/python-gitlab.cfg python-gitlab project list
```

You can register this command as an alias:

```bash
alias gitlab='docker run --rm -it -v /path/to/my/configuration/of/python-gitlab.cfg:/python-gitlab.cfg python-gitlab'
```

Which could lead to:

```bash
gitlab project list

Well, if something shows up similarly in many frameworks, one should take this into consideration. We can also consider TensorFlow XLA (https://www.tensorflow.org/performance/xla/broadcasting). XLA does not support implicit broadcasting at all; an explicit broadcasting specification is specified in the IR.

I am sensitive to the fact that adding a broadcasting op may cause more work for some frameworks. For example, Caffe2 would have to detect when the addition argument is broadcasted and fuse it into addition, to avoid materializing the broadcast. I imagine CNTK would opt to drop all of the broadcast annotations / treat them as no-ops, because their shape inference handle sit implicitly; PyTorch would probably do something similar. But we absolutely want a *simple* specification of a backend IR like ONNX, and lots of complicated broadcasting rules does not a simple language make. Better to localize the complexity in one place (e.g., a Broadcast op).

To expand on what @linkerzhang said: one straightforward way to do the check is to modify "verify" to add an extra check: if the number of actual inputs is less than the computed min-inputs, then we should verify that all the missing number of inputs at the end are "optional".

This is mostly an implementation detail (of how the check is done), so it doesn't matter much how it is done. @linkerzhang, if we let min_input be 3 in the above example, it is not unreasonable ... because it captures our rules for when an empty-string must be used. If we count the arguments specified, including empty string, we need to have a minimum of 3 to successfully pass verification (and not 2). Further, it is more efficient: instead of doing the "straightforward" check above at every node, we find how many of the trailing parameters are optional, and decrement min_input by it, once, when we compute min_input.

(BTW, the reason there is a very long list of REQUIRED_MODULES for 3.3 is that it hasn't been released yet, so changes can be made in the stdlib that could easily cause a new stdlib module to be indirectly imported by site.py. Having the full list there guards against virtualenv constantly breaking against Python's development trunk. Once 3.3 is released, the list should be narrowed to only those packages actually needed by site.py in 3.3, and the full list used only for in-development 3.4).
